title: 23 Nehase — St. Solomon the King & The Assembly
saints: St. Solomon the King and Prophet (Monthly Commemoration)
St. Irenaeus of Lyons, Bishop and Martyr
narrative:
The twenty-third day of Nehase honors St. Solomon the King and Prophet in his monthly commemoration — and the inclusion of Solomon in the Ethiopian Synaxarium carries a weight that no other Christian calendar can quite replicate, for Ethiopia's founding mythology is built around Solomon's union with the Queen of Sheba and the birth of Menelik I, who brought the Ark of the Covenant to Ethiopia. For the Ethiopian Church, Solomon is not merely a Hebrew king but a dynastic ancestor, a figure of wisdom and of divine covenant whose story intersects with Ethiopian history at the very deepest levels. His wisdom, his Temple, his Song of Songs, and his failures are all held within a tradition that has had to wrestle with him not from a comfortable distance but as a member of the family.

The Song of Songs — universally attributed to Solomon in the tradition — receives in the Ethiopian Tewahedo tradition an intensely Mariological reading: the beloved in the Song is the Virgin Mary, the Theotokos, and her divine Lover is Christ the King. This reading, coming in the post-Filseta season, resonates with particular beauty. The Church that has just celebrated the Assumption is still reading the Song: 'I am my Beloved's and my Beloved is mine.' The glorious passage of the Virgin into the fullness of eternal life is the consummation of that celestial love poem — the Bride has been taken into the Bridegroom's house, and the feast has begun. Solomon, who wrote the poem, is present today as a prophet of what he may not fully have understood.

St. Irenaeus of Lyons, the great second-century bishop and theologian who stands as one of the most important defenders of apostolic tradition in the Church's history, brings his enormous theological authority into today's assembly. Against the Gnostics who despised matter and the body, Irenaeus insisted on the goodness of creation and the materiality of the incarnation and resurrection. His phrase 'the glory of God is a living human being' is one of the most quoted in all of Christian theology, and it resonates profoundly with the feast of the Assumption: the Virgin's bodily glorification is the ultimate vindication of Irenaeus's theology. Matter matters; the body is not a cage but a temple; the resurrection of the flesh is God's final statement on His original creation.

St. Lupus the Martyr, a servant of St. Demetrius of Thessalonica who witnessed his master's martyrdom and was then himself put to death, brings the note of servant-witness into today's assembly. Lupus represents those who come to faith and to martyrdom through proximity to holiness — he was formed by what he saw in his master, and what he saw was worth dying for. Together, Solomon the wise king, Irenaeus the defender of creation's goodness, and Lupus the witness-by-proximity present the Church on this twenty-third day of Nehase with a vision of wisdom that knows where it comes from, defends what it has received, and is prepared to seal its testimony with everything it has.
